formulas.name: Doctrine of Lapse
formulas.notes: The doctrine of lapse was a policy introduced by Lord Dalhousie to annex Indian states where the ruler died without a male heir. This policy was a major contributor to the 1857 Revolt.

formulas.name: Doctrine of Discovery
formulas.notes: The doctrine of discovery was a policy that allowed the British to annex Indian states that were not being ruled effectively, or were in a state of turmoil. This doctrine was used to justify the annexation of several Indian states.

formulas.name: The Indian Act of 1858
formulas.notes: The Indian Act of 1858 was passed by the British government after the 1857 Revolt. This act restored the power to the British monarch and abolished the East India Company's rule in India.

formulas.name: The Charter Act of 1853
formulas.notes: The Charter Act of 1853 was passed by the British government to extend the charter of the East India Company for another 20 years. However, it also laid the groundwork for the eventual takeover of the East India Company's rule in India.

formulas.name: The Indian Councils Act of 1861
formulas.notes: The Indian Councils Act of 1861 was passed by the British government to reform the Indian administrative system. This act allowed for the creation of a legislative council in India, which gave Indians a greater say in the governance of the country.
